item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Took me a sec, but it's brilliant. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The whole premise of the article is a clever joke, you can look at some of the other comment after and before this to show how many people find it as a joke. It’s basically the researcher pausing time forever because he was testing an SCP. Also not all Joke SCPs have to particularly funny but instead are clever but don’t meet the standard format for regular pages. As a site moderator I assume you know some of this or have an different opinion but I’ve been on this site for awhile before I got an account and this is the usual trend when it comes to these SCPs. If you have your own explanation for why is should not be a -J please explain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, I get it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
After full 3 minutes I finally get the joke, I hate myself
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How is this a -J?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While this was definitely clever I feel like it should be in the SCP-J category rather than in the official database for a variety of reasons. The lack of info which helps this article regarding its rating does not fit the official SCP guidelines that everyone likes to use as it’s basically a empty page that’s a joke. This reminds me of the procrastination rock joke SCP which did extremely well and I think this would be better in the J category but I still gave it +1 because it was quite clever. Again probably needs to be an -J.

item.title: What means Pa-oo-say?
item.content:encoded: I got the punchline before I read the top. As soon as I saw pause I knew what was going on but then I saw "you are editing" and my mind did a double take. It's… so short and to the point. It's… like a quip. With this site you get shaggy dogs and coocoo nuts, but… That's impressive. Very nice

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
im humiliated. it took me until i looked at the page source to realize .

really good first article to read after a long time of not being on the site +1!! :]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At first I was like "Aw man, universal remote, we've all seen this before…"

And then the punchline.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So is "Dr. Archibald" Adam Sandler's nickname?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
These kinds of simple artifacts feel like the strongest forces in the SCP universe to me. Founded upon a simple law. SCP-871 duplicates if uneaten. SCP-498's alarm increases 0.4 dB per seconds. SCP-173 moves if you blink. And SCP-5510's pause button stops the world.

I wouldn't be surprised if even gods cannot alter such laws.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why? I'd imagine Junior Researchers at the Foundation would have a doctorate, either a PhD or MD, depending on their speciality. You can be both a Doctor and a Junior Researcher. Considering the inherent risk and secrecy of the job, I very much doubt the Foundation would be hiring researchers that aren't in possession of a doctorate (and realistically, they'd have at least one post-doc under their belt, too).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It works. Just take a moment to think about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Does this not work on mobile?

item.title: Please stop, or actually don't so I can stop laughing
item.content:encoded: Please stop, or actually don't so I can stop laughing!!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I will begrudgingly give this one an upvote.

It's very stupid.

But it's also funny.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At first I was like, "Bruh this is so short why does it have so many upvotes," and then I reread it and I finally got it. XD this is hilarious.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You're right, this can be a point of confusion. "«" makes things run at half speed and "»" makes things run at double speed.

I decided this was preferable than a rewind, which would have a similar "universe is stuck" kind of effect, but would be much more complicated to explain, especially through an in-progress experiment log.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: “Dr.” Means “Dr.”, not “senior researcher”

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Time was not frozen for everyone, because the article still expired due to inactivity. That is why despite it telling you you are editing it, you still have to click the edit button. The remote is “it” in a game of freeze tag

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm real dumb, how does the rewind button result in half speed….. implying that the world is being rewound at two seconds per second but still progressing forward at one second per second?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In addition to what you've mentioned, inertia and acceleration/deceleration rates would also be good indicators. If the remote is experiencing time at half speed, it would seem to have twice the inertia- that is, if you want it to move at an external speed of 1 m/s, you have to impart the force normally required to accelerate it to an internal speed of 2 m/s. At double speed, it'd only take half the force but behave like it was moving half the apparent speed (because it is).

Interactions with gravity could get especially interesting, now that I think about it, in that "answering outstanding questions in physics" way. For instance at half speed I think it would experience gravity as normal (but at half the rate) because the curvature of space-time hasn't changed, but if there's a gravitational force mediator of some sort it might experience 2 g because it recieves twice the hypothetical gravitational impulses per "tick"…

…now I have a new thought problem to play with…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Suggestion:

You could replace "Dr. Archibald" with "Junior Researcher (name)" to explain why they were being so careless with a temporal anomaly.

Somebody let a junior researcher in charge of testing a temporal anomaly, and they didn't understand that the time change experienced by the remote is actually the entire world but the remote changing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But it’s a remote control. It is, to the human eye, an inert device. If you speed up it’s time, it will sit there, twice as fast. How are you supposed to measure that? What does it mean for a remote control to go twice as fast? For any of the obvious observables, like a blinking LED or maybe its IR modulation frequency, would the researcher really describe it in these terms?
I hate everything about this. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If I thought the brevity wasn't a necessity of the idea I probably would have downvoted. It just makes it feel less substantial than I'd like it to be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To add onto this, the description reflects how they, in-universe, would measure this.

If a virtualized environment ran at double speed, it's not possible to tell within that simulation whether the flow of time has changed, since all relative measurements are unaffected, as they too are in the simulation.

The only way to tell something is off is by measuring something outside of the simulation. For instance, if a virtual machine notices the system clock has suddenly jumped. In this case, the skip experiences the same, consistent flow of time, so they notice that speeding things up (2x) makes it appear as slowed down (1/2x), and vice eversa (1/2x appears as 2x).

Solely within the environment, any statements about the objective flow of time are unfalsifiable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Look carefully at the descriptions- fast forward makes the anomaly slower.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The article was still a work-in-progress, so you could argue that would make sense in-universe. Besides, what else do you need to know? Drawing it out could ruin the "punchline". I think the brevity makes it even better- they were in the process of testing it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, agreed. Unless of course, the effect we're seeing on the anomaly is just a result of it influencing reality. In which case, it's a matter of perspective.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconding this.

item.title: It's short and silly...
item.content:encoded:
…but I'm a sucker for creative articles.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At first I expected a ¨make your own article¨ type of skip. And then it hit me…… +1, paused forever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When one is pressed, the subjective time experienced by the anomaly changes.

This would have made a lot more sense if it used a different word. As it stands… ok, sure, the remote control is paused. Fine? I guess? The fact that the anomaly is specifically mentioned here implies that only the anomaly is affected and not all of reality.

item.title: Astonishing
item.content:encoded: This is a perfectly executed piece of metafiction. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A temporal anomaly being handled so carelessly is dubious to me, Safe status or no; so personal no-vote, but acknowledgement that it's an excellent stinger.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Only for him or for other people as well? I mean, the entire humanity is stuck in time forever because of a scientist's mistake? Man, this scp is so delicious evil.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can tell that you purposely shortened the SCP to make the joke work. While I can admire that, it’s also a problem in and of itself. There’s not really much to talk about with this SCP. It doesn’t really stick with me at all, and it’s like eating a single raisin out of a tub of raisins, then realizing you're out of raisins.

I’m upvoting because it works and it’s the first short scp I’ve seen that actually works in forever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: He paused.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Brilliance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This got to me way more than it should have.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's ok, just wait for the batteries to run out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This would probably be a ZK-Class ("Reality Failure") scenario. And yeah, I don't imagine any recovery from this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So basically a XK-Class scenario was started because of an remote? Time is frozen forever?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't get it. Can someone explain to me what happened to dr Archibald when he pressed pause, please? Did he turned into a cursor or he got stuck in time? Thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: finally, a short article worthy of upvoting. this is fucking awesome. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Lmao

I’ve legit been thinking of doing an article with a punchline like this. Glad to see this one survive!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Click SCP, starring Archibald Sanders
Damn, this is brutal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I waited 118 seconds for something on the page to change. Nothing happened. I then waited another 118 seconds, without switching tabs, going to a different programme…nothing happened. I then re-read the article.

You got me.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This joke is incredibly stupid and yet I laughed. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is pretty cool cause I

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was waiting for the punchline and then it hit me like a train. This is so evil, take your upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: bwahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahahaha +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I find it extremely suspect that Archibald didn't think twice about pressing pause but can excuse it because I adore this kind of punchline.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
No, it's not incomplete.

This has been sitting in my sandbox for a long time. I figured I should just post it and see if it sinks or swims.

Thanks a lot to my valued critiquers for their feedback:

  • wctaiwan
  • Rounderhouse
  • The Great Hippo
  • Uncle Nicolini
  • Yossipossi
  • Captain Kirby

By my count this article is 104 words.

+ Spoiler
- Spoiler

The final character is an unblinking cursor.

+ Image Credits
- Image Credits
  • blank.png is from SCP-4182
  • cursor.png is a screenshot of the character "𝖨"
